// Service Provider Technologies · Cisco MPLS

MULTIPROTOCOL
LABEL SWITCHING

A deep-dive interactive guide covering MPLS fundamentals, VPN types (L2 & L3), header structure, Cisco configuration, troubleshooting flowcharts, and verification commands — everything you need to understand how modern ISP networks operate.

32bits per label
2.5Layer number
50msFRR failover
1M+label values
01
// Introduction
What Is MPLS?

MPLS (Multiprotocol Label Switching) is a packet-forwarding technique invented in the late 1990s that routes traffic using short fixed-length labels rather than performing a full IP destination lookup at every router hop. It operates at Layer 2.5 — sitting between the data-link and network layers.

ANIMATED — A Packet Crossing the MPLS Core (PUSH → SWAP → POP)
PUSH → SWAP → POP: the edge router (PE-1) pushes a label, core routers (P) swap it hop-by-hop, then PE-2 pops the label, leaving a plain IP packet.
The Problem with Traditional IP Routing
When a router receives a packet it must:
  1. Read the full destination IP address
  2. Perform a Longest-Prefix Match (LPM) lookup in the routing table
  3. Forward to next-hop
At millions of packets/second in an ISP core, this is CPU-intensive and slow — especially with large routing tables.
How MPLS Solves It
MPLS replaces IP lookups in the core with a simple label swap:
  1. Ingress PE: Attaches a short 32-bit label to the packet (PUSH)
  2. Core P routers: Swap label and forward — no IP lookup needed (SWAP)
  3. Egress PE: Removes the label, delivers original IP packet (POP)
Label lookups can be done in hardware (ASIC), making forwarding extremely fast.
Label Edge Router (LER / PE): Sits at the boundary of the MPLS network. Ingress LER pushes labels; Egress LER pops them.
Label Switch Router (LSR / P): Core router. Only reads the top label and swaps it — never inspects the IP payload.
Label Switched Path (LSP): The unidirectional logical tunnel from Ingress PE through all P routers to Egress PE.
LDP (Label Distribution Protocol): The control-plane protocol that distributes label-to-FEC bindings between LSRs automatically.
FEC (Forwarding Equivalence Class): A group of packets forwarded the same way — typically a destination IP prefix. Each FEC gets a label.
PHP (Penultimate Hop Popping): The second-to-last router pops the outer label before sending to the Egress PE, reducing processing load.
02
// Applications
What Is MPLS Used For?

MPLS evolved from a speed technology into a multi-service platform. Today's primary use cases are VPN services, Traffic Engineering, and Fast Reroute protection — with the original speed motivation now secondary to CEF.

● MOST COMMON USE CASE

MPLS VPN — Private WAN Services

Service providers use MPLS to offer enterprise customers a private WAN across shared physical infrastructure. Each customer's traffic is completely separated, even though they share the same core network — similar conceptually to the old Frame Relay Virtual Circuits but far more scalable.

Key mechanism: Two label layers (outer transport label + inner VPN label) carry customer traffic. VRF tables on PE routers isolate each customer's routing information completely.
Why Enterprises Choose MPLS VPN
✓ Any-to-Any connectivity — no hub-and-spoke limitations

✓ Overlapping IP space — two customers can both use 10.0.0.0/8; VRF + RD keeps them separate

✓ Managed routing — SP handles routing between branches (L3VPN), or simply extends Layer 2 (L2VPN)

✓ SLA guarantees — bandwidth reservation and guaranteed paths

✓ Scalable — P routers carry only transport labels; customer routes stay on PE only
● TRAFFIC MANAGEMENT

MPLS Traffic Engineering (MPLS-TE)

Traditional IP routing always follows the shortest path, causing some links to be congested while parallel links sit idle. MPLS-TE allows network engineers to define explicit paths and reserve bandwidth along those paths.

Uses RSVP-TE (Resource Reservation Protocol — Traffic Engineering) or newer Segment Routing to establish TE tunnels with guaranteed bandwidth.
TE Use Cases
Load balancing across parallel links — direct internet traffic over backup links when primary is congested

Priority traffic paths — force voice, video, or critical control traffic onto the most stable, low-latency path

Constraint-based routing — avoid certain links, maximize bandwidth utilization across the entire network

WAN optimization — ISPs can maximize ROI on expensive backbone infrastructure
● HIGH AVAILABILITY

MPLS Fast Reroute (FRR)

When a fiber link breaks, normal IP routing needs seconds (or minutes) for OSPF/BGP to reconverge. MPLS Fast Reroute pre-computes a backup path and stores it in hardware. Failover happens in < 50 milliseconds — invisible to voice calls and real-time applications.

Critical for services with SLAs: Voice over IP, video conferencing, financial trading systems, radar/aviation data feeds — anything that cannot tolerate multi-second outages.
FRR vs. IP Convergence
Recovery timeMPLS FRR: <50msIP: 1–30 seconds
MechanismPre-computed backup LSPSPF recomputation
VoIP impactImperceptibleCall drops
SLA compliance99.999%+ achievableDifficult to guarantee
● HISTORICAL + QOS

Speed Origin & QoS

MPLS was originally designed to accelerate IP forwarding. Today, Cisco CEF (Cisco Express Forwarding) handles IP at hardware speed, making MPLS's speed advantage less critical. However, the TC/EXP field (3 bits) in the MPLS header enables DiffServ QoS across the MPLS domain — marking packets with priority class 0–7 for differentiated treatment.

The MPLS TC field maps directly to IP DSCP/Precedence values, enabling end-to-end QoS policies across the SP network without exposing customer IP headers.
QoS Priority Classes (TC bits)
TC=7Network Control (OSPF, LDP)
TC=6Voice (EF — Expedited Forwarding)
TC=5Video Conferencing
TC=4Call Signaling
TC=3–1Business critical data
TC=0Best-effort internet
03
// VPN Types
MPLS VPN Types: L3VPN vs L2VPN

MPLS supports two fundamentally different VPN models. The key difference is where the SP participates in the customer's routing — at Layer 3 (IP routing) or only at Layer 2 (transparent pipe).

ANIMATED — VRF Isolating Two Customers With the Same IP Address Space
Customer-A and Customer-B both use 10.0.0.0/8, but their Route Distinguishers (100:1 vs 100:2) make them globally unique VPNv4 addresses — they never mix.

Layer 3 MPLS VPN (L3VPN — RFC 4364)

The SP's PE routers participate in the customer's routing. PE routers maintain separate VRF (Virtual Routing and Forwarding) tables per customer, exchange routes via MP-BGP, and prepend a Route Distinguisher (RD) to make overlapping IP prefixes globally unique (VPNv4 addresses).

CE Router: Customer Edge — connects to PE, runs normal IP routing (no MPLS needed on CE)
PE Router: Provider Edge — maintains per-customer VRF, runs MP-BGP, pushes 2 label layers
P Router: Provider Core — only swaps transport labels; never sees customer routes
L3VPN Two-Label Stack
L3VPN uses two MPLS labels stacked together:

Outer Label (Transport) — assigned by LDP. Routes the packet from Ingress PE to Egress PE through the P core. Swapped at each P router.

Inner Label (VPN / BGP) — assigned by MP-BGP. Tells the Egress PE which customer VRF this belongs to and which interface to deliver it out.

When the Egress PE receives the packet, the outer label has already been popped (PHP), and the inner VPN label is used for VRF lookup.

Layer 2 MPLS VPN (L2VPN)

The SP acts as a "giant transparent switch". It transports Layer 2 frames (Ethernet, with MAC addresses and VLAN tags preserved) across the MPLS core. The PE router does not participate in the customer's IP routing — the customer manages their own IP layer.

Point-to-Point L2VPN (VPWS / AToM)
Virtual Private Wire Service — emulates a dedicated leased line between exactly 2 customer sites.

Cisco calls this Any Transport over MPLS (AToM).

✓ No MAC learning on PE — PE simply receives Frame from Port A, stamps a pseudowire label, sends to Port B

✓ Supports Ethernet, Frame Relay, ATM, PPP, HDLC transported transparently over MPLS

Best for: Data Center interconnect, backup links, connecting 2 branch offices directly
Multipoint L2VPN (VPLS)
Virtual Private LAN Service — emulates a giant Ethernet switch connecting all customer sites as if they were on one LAN.

PE routers maintain MAC address tables (VFI) per customer and flood BUM traffic (Broadcast/Unknown/Multicast).

✓ All sites share one IP subnet — all branches can use 192.168.1.0/24

✓ Customer owns all routing — can run OSPF, EIGRP internally

Best for: CCTV systems across city, bank ATM networks, any application needing Layer 2 across WAN

L3VPN vs L2VPN Comparison

FeatureL3VPN (RFC 4364)L2VPN VPWS (AToM)L2VPN VPLS
SP roleIP routing participantTransparent wireTransparent switch
Label stack2 labels (outer + VPN)2 labels (outer + PW)2 labels (outer + PW)
MAC learning on PENot applicableNo — wire onlyYes — VFI table
Routing protocol on CEOSPF/BGP/EIGRP → PECustomer manages own routingCustomer manages own routing
Overlapping IPsHandled via RD/RTCustomer's problemCustomer's problem
TopologyAny (full mesh, hub-spoke)Point-to-Point onlyMultipoint (any-to-any)
Use caseEnterprise WAN with many branchesDC interconnect, leased line replacementATM networks, CCTV, LAN extension
Cisco command basisvrf + mp-bgpl2vpn xconnectvfi + bridge-domain
04
// Architecture
Structure of MPLS

The MPLS header is a compact 32-bit shim inserted between the Layer 2 header and the Layer 3 (IP) header. Multiple labels can be stacked (label stacking).

ANIMATED — Walking Through the Four Label Fields (Label → TC → S → TTL)
Fields reveal slowly so you can follow along: Label (20 bits) → TC/EXP (3 bits) → S/BoS (1 bit) → TTL (8 bits).
// DIAGRAM — MPLS 32-bit Label Structure & Position in Frame
The MPLS label sits between L2 (Ethernet) and L3 (IP). The outer label is at the top of the stack; inner labels (if stacked) follow below it.

The 4 Fields — Click to Explore

LABEL
20 bits
TC
3 bits
S
1 bit
TTL
8 bits
LABEL Field — 20 bits (bits 0–19)
The actual forwarding label used by LSRs to make switching decisions. Supports 2²⁰ = 1,048,576 unique label values.

Reserved labels (0–15): Label 0 = IPv4 Explicit Null, Label 3 = Implicit Null (PHP trigger), Labels 4–15 reserved by IANA.

Working range: 16 – 1,048,575. Each router independently allocates labels from this range — labels are locally significant only.
TC (Traffic Class) / EXP Field — 3 bits (bits 20–22)
Originally called "EXP" (Experimental), now formally renamed Traffic Class (TC) by RFC 5462.

Used for QoS marking within the MPLS domain. Values 0–7 map to IP DSCP/Precedence values at domain boundaries.

Allows differentiated treatment: TC=6 for voice (highest priority), TC=0 for best-effort internet.
S (Bottom of Stack) — 1 bit (bit 23)
Critical flag for label stacking. Since multiple MPLS labels can be stacked on a single packet (e.g. L3VPN = 2 labels, TE = 3 labels), this bit tells the router whether more labels follow below.

S = 0: More labels below — keep reading the stack.
S = 1: This is the bottom-most label — next bytes are the IP header (or other L3 payload).
TTL (Time to Live) — 8 bits (bits 24–31)
Works identically to the IP TTL field. Decremented by 1 at each hop. If it reaches 0, the packet is discarded and an ICMP Time Exceeded message is sent back.

Purpose: Prevents routing loops from causing infinite packet circulation in the network.

Note: On ingress, the IP TTL is typically copied into the MPLS TTL. On egress, the MPLS TTL is copied back to the IP TTL (this behavior can be disabled for security — "no mpls ip propagate-ttl").
// DIAGRAM — Label Allocation (Local Significance)
Each router in the MPLS domain independently allocates locally-significant labels. LDP distributes these bindings to neighbors — Router A assigns label 21 for Network X, Router B assigns 25, Router C assigns 34. Each hop knows its in-label/out-label pair, forming the complete LSP.
// DIAGRAM — Label Switching Router (LSR) — Label Allocation Across the Domain
Each router in the MPLS domain allocates locally-significant labels. LDP distributes these bindings to neighbors, building an end-to-end LSP where each hop knows its in-label/out-label pair.
ANIMATED — LDP Session Establishment (Hello → Init → Label Bindings)
Steps: discover neighbors (Hello multicast) → establish TCP session (Init/Open) → advertise label bindings (FEC → label mapping messages).

MTU Consideration — Critical in Practice

// DIAGRAM — MTU Frame Sizes: IP-MTU, MPLS-MTU, L2-MTU, Full Frame
Each MPLS label adds 4 bytes. L3VPN stacks 2 labels = 8 bytes overhead. If interfaces remain at 1500-byte MTU, this causes fragmentation or packet drops when DF bit is set.
⚠ Common deployment mistake: Forgetting to increase MTU on interfaces and core switches when deploying MPLS. Each label = 4 bytes. L3VPN (2 labels) = 8 bytes extra. Set mpls mtu 1508 on router interfaces and enable system mtu jumbo 9000 on core switches that support it.
05
// Implementation
How to Configure MPLS

MPLS configuration builds in layers: first enable the core (IGP + LDP), then add VPN services (VRF + MP-BGP for L3VPN, or pseudowires for L2VPN).

// DIAGRAM — Configuration Scenario Topology (PE1–P1–P2–PE2)
Reference topology: PE1 ↔ P1 ↔ P2 ↔ PE2 with 192.168.101.0/24 network reachable via PE2. MPLS must be enabled on all core-facing interfaces.
// DIAGRAM — Basic MPLS LDP Interface Configuration
On IOS XR platforms, use "mpls ldp" global config mode and list interfaces. On IOS/IOS-XE, enable "mpls ip" directly under each interface.

STEP 1 — Configure IGP (OSPF) with loopbacks

ALL ROUTERS — IGP Foundation
Router(config)# router ospf 1 Router(config-router)# router-id 1.1.1.1 ! Use loopback IP as stable router-id Router(config-router)# network 0.0.0.0 255.255.255.255 area 0 ! Advertise all interfaces Router(config-router)# mpls ldp sync ! Prevent traffic black-holes during LDP convergence

STEP 2 — Enable MPLS/LDP on interfaces (IOS/IOS-XE)

P1 ROUTER — IOS/IOS-XE Platform
P1(config)# mpls ip ! Enable MPLS globally (some platforms) P1(config)# mpls label protocol ldp ! Use LDP for label distribution P1(config)# interface GigabitEthernet0/0/0/0 P1(config-if)# mpls ip ! Enable MPLS on this interface P1(config)# interface GigabitEthernet0/0/0/1 P1(config-if)# mpls ip P1(config-if)# mpls mtu 1508 ! Increase MTU for label overhead

STEP 2 (alt) — Enable MPLS/LDP (IOS-XR Platform)

P1 ROUTER — IOS-XR Platform
RP/0/0/CPU0:P1(config)# mpls ldp RP/0/0/CPU0:P1(config-ldp)# interface GigabitEthernet0/0/0/0 RP/0/0/CPU0:P1(config-ldp-if)# ! RP/0/0/CPU0:P1(config-ldp)# interface GigabitEthernet0/0/0/1 RP/0/0/CPU0:P1(config-ldp)# commit

L3VPN requires: VRF definition (with RD and RT) on PE routers, MP-BGP session between PEs, and CE-PE routing protocol. The P routers need no additional configuration.

STEP 1 — Define VRF on PE routers

PE1 — VRF Definition
PE1(config)# vrf definition CUSTOMER-A PE1(config-vrf)# rd 100:1 ! Route Distinguisher — makes routes globally unique PE1(config-vrf)# address-family ipv4 PE1(config-vrf-af)# route-target export 100:1 ! RT to attach when exporting routes PE1(config-vrf-af)# route-target import 100:1 ! RT to accept when importing routes

STEP 2 — Bind CE-facing interface to VRF

PE1 — Interface in VRF
PE1(config)# interface GigabitEthernet0/1 ! CE-facing interface PE1(config-if)# vrf forwarding CUSTOMER-A PE1(config-if)# ip address 192.168.10.1 255.255.255.0 PE1(config-if)# no shutdown

STEP 3 — Configure MP-BGP between PE routers

PE1 — MP-BGP Configuration
PE1(config)# router bgp 65000 PE1(config-router)# bgp router-id 1.1.1.1 ! Use loopback address PE1(config-router)# no bgp default ipv4-unicast ! Disable default IPv4 AF for VPN peers PE1(config-router)# neighbor 4.4.4.4 remote-as 65000 ! iBGP to PE2 (use loopback) PE1(config-router)# neighbor 4.4.4.4 update-source Loopback0 PE1(config-router)# address-family vpnv4 ! Enable VPNv4 address family PE1(config-router-af)# neighbor 4.4.4.4 activate PE1(config-router-af)# neighbor 4.4.4.4 send-community extended

STEP 4 — CE-PE Routing (OSPF example)

PE1 — CE-PE OSPF in VRF Context
PE1(config)# router ospf 100 vrf CUSTOMER-A PE1(config-router)# network 192.168.10.0 0.0.0.255 area 0 PE1(config-router)# redistribute bgp 65000 subnets ! Bring BGP routes into customer OSPF PE1(config)# router bgp 65000 PE1(config-router)# address-family ipv4 vrf CUSTOMER-A PE1(config-router-af)# redistribute ospf 100 ! OSPF routes into VPN BGP

AToM / VPWS — Point-to-Point Pseudowire (IOS)

PE1 — EoMPLS Pseudowire (legacy xconnect)
PE1(config)# interface GigabitEthernet0/1 PE1(config-if)# xconnect 4.4.4.4 100 encapsulation mpls ! 4.4.4.4 = remote PE2 loopback ! 100 = VC-ID (must match on both PEs) ! Creates pseudowire to PE2 for customer 100

AToM — New L2VPN CLI (IOS-XE)

PE1 — L2VPN Protocol-Based CLI
PE1(config)# l2vpn xconnect context CUST-A-PW PE1(config-xc)# member GigabitEthernet0/1 service-instance 1 PE1(config-xc)# member pseudowire 1 PE1(config)# interface pseudowire 1 PE1(config-if)# encapsulation mpls PE1(config-if)# neighbor 4.4.4.4 100 ! VC-ID 100

VPLS — Multipoint L2VPN

PE1 — VPLS Configuration
PE1(config)# l2 vfi CUST-VPLS manual PE1(config-vfi)# vpn id 200 PE1(config-vfi)# neighbor 4.4.4.4 encapsulation mpls PE1(config-vfi)# neighbor 3.3.3.3 encapsulation mpls ! Add all PE neighbors sharing this VPLS PE1(config)# interface GigabitEthernet0/1 PE1(config-if)# switchport PE1(config-if)# switchport mode access PE1(config-if)# xconnect vfi CUST-VPLS
Auto-discovery VPLS (BGP-based): Use l2 vfi CUST-VPLS autodiscovery bgp to have PE routers discover VPLS members automatically via BGP — no manual neighbor listing needed.
ANIMATED — CLI Configuration Walkthrough (LDP core → L3VPN → L2VPN pseudowire)
Watch each command typed out live: OSPF/LDP core setup → VRF + RD/RT for L3VPN → MP-BGP VPNv4 peering → L2VPN xconnect pseudowire.

Verification Commands — Build-Up Order

VERIFICATION SEQUENCE — Run In This Order
! Step 1: IGP working? PE1# show ip ospf neighbor ! All neighbors in FULL state? PE1# show ip route ospf ! Loopbacks of all PEs reachable? ! Step 2: MPLS interfaces enabled? PE1# show mpls interfaces ! Shows "Yes" in LDP column for each interface ! Step 3: LDP sessions established? PE1# show mpls ldp neighbor ! All neighbors in OPERATIONAL state PE1# show mpls ldp discovery ! Verify Hello messages being sent/received ! Step 4: Labels being assigned? PE1# show mpls ldp bindings ! Shows local/remote label bindings per prefix ! Step 5: Forwarding table populated? PE1# show mpls forwarding-table ! LFIB — in-label, out-label, next-hop ! For L3VPN — additional steps: PE1# show bgp vpnv4 unicast all summary ! MP-BGP peers in Established state? PE1# show ip route vrf CUSTOMER-A ! VRF routing table populated? PE1# show bgp vpnv4 unicast all ! VPNv4 routes received from peers? PE1# ping vrf CUSTOMER-A 192.168.20.1 ! End-to-end connectivity test PE1# traceroute vrf CUSTOMER-A 192.168.20.1
06
// Reference
MPLS Command Reference

Quick-reference table of the most important Cisco MPLS show commands and their purpose.

CommandWhat It ShowsWhen to Use
show mpls interfacesWhich interfaces have MPLS/LDP enabledFirst check — verify MPLS enabled on correct ports
show mpls ldp neighborActive LDP sessions, peer addresses, stateVerify LDP sessions are OPERATIONAL
show mpls ldp discoveryLDP Hello discovery status per interfaceDebug why LDP session won't form
show mpls ldp bindingsLocal and remote label bindings per FEC/prefixVerify labels are being distributed for all prefixes
show mpls forwarding-tableLFIB — in-label, out-label, outgoing interface, next-hopVerify data plane has correct forwarding entries
show mpls forwarding-table [prefix]LFIB entry for a specific destination prefixDebug end-to-end path for specific traffic
show mpls ldp bindings [prefix]Label bindings for a specific FECVerify label for specific prefix matches expected
show ip vrfAll VRF instances configured on PE, interfaces assignedVerify VRF setup and interface membership
show ip route vrf [name]Routing table for a specific VRFVerify customer routes are in VRF table
show bgp vpnv4 unicast all summaryAll MP-BGP VPNv4 peer states and prefix countsVerify MP-BGP sessions are up and exchanging routes
show bgp vpnv4 unicast allFull BGP VPNv4 table with RD, RT, labelsDeep-dive VPN route details and label allocation
show bgp vpnv4 unicast vrf [name] [prefix]Specific VPN prefix detailsVerify single prefix RT, label, next-hop
show xconnect allAll L2VPN pseudowire states (AToM/VPLS)Verify L2VPN pseudowires are UP
show l2vpn atom vc detailAToM pseudowire details, labels, statusDebug L2VPN circuit issues
show vfi [name]VPLS VFI instance details and member peersVerify VPLS configuration and peer reachability
debug mpls ldp sessionsReal-time LDP session negotiation messagesDebug LDP session formation issues (use carefully)
show route ping [addr]Test reachability to a destinationVerify LSP path works end-to-end
traceroute mpls ipv4 [prefix/len]Label-by-label traceroute through the MPLS domainTrace exact LSP path, verify PHP behavior
Troubleshooting order: Always verify in sequence: ospf neighbor → mpls interfaces → ldp neighbor → ldp bindings → forwarding-table → (for VPN) bgp vpnv4 summary → ip route vrf. Each step depends on the previous one.
07
// Diagnostics
MPLS Troubleshooting Flowchart
// FLOWCHART — Label Redistribution Troubleshooting (LDP Discovery → Session → Labels)
Start from "Labels not redistributed" — verify LDP discovery → check LDP session → verify loopback reachability → resolve accordingly.

Step-by-Step Troubleshooting Guide

1
Verify IGP is running and loopbacks are reachable
show ip ospf neighbor show ip route 4.4.4.4
All PE and P router loopbacks MUST be reachable via IGP before LDP can form. LDP uses loopback addresses for TCP sessions. If IGP is broken, nothing else will work.
2
Verify MPLS is enabled on correct interfaces
show mpls interfaces
The output should show "Yes" in the "LDP" column for all core-facing interfaces. If MPLS is not enabled on an interface, LDP Hellos will not be sent/received on that link.
3
Verify LDP discovery (Hello messages)
show mpls ldp discovery debug mpls ldp transport events
LDP Hello messages are sent to the multicast address 224.0.0.2 on each MPLS-enabled interface. If discovery is not working, check interface status, MTU, and MPLS configuration on both sides of the link.
4
Verify LDP TCP session is OPERATIONAL
show mpls ldp neighbor show mpls ldp neighbor detail
LDP uses TCP port 646 for session establishment. If discovery works but session won't form: check that loopback addresses are reachable (ping 4.4.4.4 from loopback source), check ACLs blocking TCP 646, verify no authentication mismatch.
5
Verify labels are being distributed (bindings)
show mpls ldp bindings show mpls ldp bindings 192.168.1.0/24
Should show both local label (lib entry with "out") and remote labels (learned from neighbor "in"). If bindings are missing for some prefixes, check that the prefix is in the routing table: LDP only distributes labels for IGP routes.
6
Verify LFIB forwarding table is populated
show mpls forwarding-table show mpls forwarding-table 4.4.4.4/32
The LFIB maps in-labels to out-labels and outgoing interfaces. If a prefix appears in LDP bindings but not the LFIB, the route may have been withdrawn from the routing table or there's a CEF issue. Try "clear ip route *" and check again.
7
[L3VPN] Verify MP-BGP VPNv4 sessions
show bgp vpnv4 unicast all summary show bgp vpnv4 unicast all neighbors 4.4.4.4
MP-BGP sessions should show state "Established" with non-zero prefix counts. If session is down: check TCP 179 reachability between loopbacks, verify ASN matches, check "address-family vpnv4" is activated for the neighbor, verify "send-community extended" is configured.
8
[L3VPN] Verify VRF routing table has customer routes
show ip route vrf CUSTOMER-A show bgp vpnv4 unicast vrf CUSTOMER-A
Customer routes should appear in the VRF routing table. If routes are missing: verify Route Targets match (import RT on one PE must equal export RT on the other). Check "show bgp vpnv4 unicast all" for RT values. Also verify CE-PE routing protocol is exchanging prefixes correctly.
9
End-to-end connectivity test
ping vrf CUSTOMER-A 192.168.20.1 traceroute vrf CUSTOMER-A 192.168.20.1 traceroute mpls ipv4 4.4.4.4/32
Use extended ping/traceroute within the VRF context. The MPLS traceroute shows label-by-label hops and can reveal where in the LSP packets are being dropped or misrouted.
!
MTU / Fragmentation Issues
ping vrf CUST size 1500 df-bit show interfaces GigabitEthernet0/0 | inc MTU
Symptom: large pings fail but small ones succeed, or web browsing works but file transfers hang. Cause: MPLS label overhead (4-8 bytes) causes packets to exceed interface MTU. Fix: set "mpls mtu 1508" on router interfaces and "system mtu jumbo" on core switches.
08
